Summary: PU.1 (SPI1) functions as a master regulator of macrophage and microglia development in mammals and teleosts. PU.1 controls expression of genes required for macrophage and microglia development. Loss of PU.1 leads to downregulation of Csf1r and loss of anti-apoptotic genes such as BCL2A1. ChIP-Seq in BV2 cells revealed thousands of PU.1 targets. Disruption of PU.1 in mice causes embryonic lethality and complete absence of macrophages including microglia. Pu.1-null zebrafish completely lack macrophage and microglia development at early stages. PU.1 dosage specifies survival and maintenance of microglia in teleosts and mammals. Conditional ablation of PU.1 in mice or Pu.1/Spi-b in zebrafish produces rapid depletion of microglia. Silencing PU.1 by siRNA in mixed human glial culture reduced microglial viability and decreased phagocytosis of amyloid-beta (Aβ42). Conditional or pharmacological inhibition of PU.1 in an AD mouse model ameliorated neuroinflammation, prevented microgliosis, and improved cognition.
References: Wu, Y., Guo, W., Kuang, H., Wu, X., Trinh, T. H., Wang, Y., Zhao, S., Wen, Z., & Yu, T. (2025). Pu.1/Spi1 dosage controls the turnover and maintenance of microglia in zebrafish and mammals. Elife, 14, RP105788. https://doi.org/10.7554/elife.105788
